heroOverview: Levocardia is a congenital condition in which the heart occupies its normal left-sided position in the chest, yet the rest of the abdominal organs are mirror-imaged or randomly arranged — a combination called situs inversus or situs ambiguus with levocardia. It affects roughly 1 in 22,000 live births and is almost always accompanied by complex structural heart defects that require surgical correction. Unlike simple dextrocardia, where the heart itself is flipped, levocardia represents a mismatch between cardiac position and visceral arrangement, which is why nearly all affected individuals have significant cardiac malformations such as transposition of the great vessels, single ventricle, or pulmonary stenosis. Early echocardiographic diagnosis is essential because the associated defects determine life expectancy and guide the timing of palliative or corrective surgery.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 22,000 live births; represents fewer than 1% of all congenital heart disease
keyStats.demographics: Equally distributed between males and females; most cases are sporadic with no clear family history
keyStats.avgAge: Diagnosed in the neonatal period or early infancy; prenatal detection increasingly possible with fetal echocardiography
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated several hundred new cases per year worldwide given global birth rates

definition: Levocardia (ICD-10: Q24.1) describes the cardiac anatomical situation in which the heart sits in the normal left hemithorax — the cardiac apex pointing to the left — yet the visceral situs (the arrangement of the abdominal organs) is abnormal. Under normal circumstances the heart, stomach, and spleen are left-sided while the liver is right-sided; this concordant arrangement is called situs solitus. When the abdominal organs are completely mirror-imaged (situs inversus totalis), the thoracic and abdominal arrangements remain concordant and the heart is usually structurally normal. Isolated levocardia arises when the abdominal organs adopt the mirror-image arrangement (situs inversus) or a heterotaxic pattern (situs ambiguus, as in asplenia or polysplenia syndromes) while the heart retains its left-sided position, creating a discordant relationship that is almost invariably associated with complex intracardiac anomalies. The malformations most frequently seen include transposition of the great arteries, pulmonary stenosis or atresia, hypoplastic right ventricle, atrioventricular septal defects, and total anomalous pulmonary venous connection. From a surgical standpoint, levocardia with situs ambiguus (heterotaxy) carries a particularly high burden of associated anomalies including asplenia (and consequent immunodeficiency) or polysplenia (often with interrupted inferior vena cava). The condition falls under the subspecialty of paediatric or congenital cardiology and almost always requires multidisciplinary management.

typesClassification.description: The abdominal organs are mirror-imaged (liver on the left, stomach and spleen on the right) while the heart remains in the left hemithorax. Complex intracardiac defects are nearly universal.

typesClassification.type: Levocardia with situs ambiguus — asplenia syndrome
typesClassification.description: Bilateral right-sidedness (right isomerism): two morphologically right atria, absence of the spleen, midline liver, and frequently total anomalous pulmonary venous connection and pulmonary stenosis or atresia. Asplenia creates severe immunodeficiency.

typesClassification.type: Levocardia with situs ambiguus — polysplenia syndrome
typesClassification.description: Bilateral left-sidedness (left isomerism): multiple small spleens, interrupted inferior vena cava with azygos continuation, and bilateral bilobed lungs. Cardiac defects are usually less severe than in asplenia.

typesClassification.type: Levocardia with situs solitus — incidental cardiac malposition
typesClassification.description: Rarely, situs is solitus but minor cardiac rotation creates apparent levocardia; structural defects may still be present and require evaluation.

primarySymptoms: Central cyanosis — a blue tinge of the lips, tongue, and nail beds present from birth when there is reduced pulmonary blood flow or transposition of the great arteries.
Respiratory distress at birth, with fast, laboured breathing and rib recession, reflecting inadequate cardiac output or pulmonary overcirculation.
Poor feeding and failure to thrive in the first weeks of life; neonates tire rapidly during feeds because of the increased cardiac workload.
Hepatomegaly detectable on abdominal examination, reflecting raised right-sided filling pressures or systemic venous congestion.

Cardiac murmur on auscultation, the nature of which depends on the specific structural defect — most commonly a harsh ejection murmur from pulmonary stenosis or a pansystolic murmur from a ventricular septal defect.
Abnormal heart sounds including a single loud S2 when only one great artery is functional, or widely split S2 in the setting of right bundle-branch block.
Repeated lower respiratory tract infections in infancy and childhood due to increased pulmonary blood flow or aspiration related to feeding difficulties.
Oedema or ascites in older infants if ventricular dysfunction and heart failure develop without surgical intervention.
Clubbing of the fingers and toes emerging over months in children with chronic cyanosis or reduced oxygen saturation.
Palpitations or arrhythmias, particularly in the setting of heterotaxy where conduction system anatomy is abnormal.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent oxygen saturation below 95% on pulse oximetry screening in the newborn period.
A murmur heard by a midwife or neonatologist prompting echocardiography before discharge.
Mild central cyanosis noted in room air that worsens with crying.
Prenatal four-chamber view anomaly detected on routine obstetric ultrasound, leading to fetal echocardiography.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe cyanosis with collapse in a neonate — possible duct-dependent circulation with ductal closure requiring urgent prostaglandin E1.
Hypercyanotic spell (tet spell) with uncontrollable crying, extreme blueness, and loss of consciousness in an infant with pulmonary stenosis.
Signs of low cardiac output: extreme pallor, poor peripheral perfusion, cool extremities, metabolic acidosis.
Septic shock in an asplenic child who has not received prophylactic antibiotics or vaccines — a life-threatening emergency.
Rapidly increasing respiratory distress with pulmonary oedema from high-flow congenital lesions.

causes.cause: Defective left-right axis determination during embryogenesis
causes.description: Between days 18 and 28 of gestation, a cascade of laterality genes — including NODAL, LEFTY, PITX2, and CFC1 — establishes which structures develop on which side. Mutations or abnormal ciliary function disrupt this cascade, allowing the heart to default to left-sided position while visceral patterning deviates.

Motile cilia at the embryonic node generate a leftward fluid flow that is the first physical signal of left-right asymmetry. Loss-of-function mutations in genes such as DNAI1, DNAI2, and DNAH5 paralyse these cilia, randomising visceral situs in roughly 50% of affected individuals and occasionally producing levocardia with situs inversus.

NODAL, a TGF-beta family member, and its co-receptors and antagonists (LEFTY1, LEFTY2, CRIPTO) are expressed asymmetrically on the left side of the developing embryo. Heterozygous or compound heterozygous variants in these genes have been found in familial and sporadic cases of heterotaxy including levocardia.

Variants in ZIC3, located on the X chromosome, account for approximately 1% of all heterotaxy cases and represent the most common identified genetic cause of X-linked congenital heart disease. Males are predominantly affected and present with complex heterotaxy including levocardia.

First-trimester exposure to certain teratogens — including maternal diabetes mellitus, retinoic acid excess, and some anticonvulsants — has been associated with laterality defects. Maternal pregestational diabetes carries a two- to fourfold increased risk of complex congenital heart disease including heterotaxy.

Levocardia can occur as part of broader chromosomal syndromes; trisomy 18 and trisomy 13 are associated with cardiac and visceral malposition. Non-immune hydrops fetalis from cardiac failure may accelerate presentation.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of heterotaxy or primary ciliary dyskinesia
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of individuals with heterotaxy have a recurrence risk estimated at 3-10% compared with the population baseline; this rises further if a causative mutation in NODAL, ZIC3, or a ciliary gene is identified.

Poorly controlled maternal diabetes in the first trimester raises the risk of several congenital heart defects including heterotaxy by two- to fourfold, likely through oxidative stress affecting laterality gene expression.

riskFactors.factor: Maternal exposure to retinoic acid or isotretinoin
riskFactors.description: Excess retinoic acid signalling during the embryonic period disrupts the NODAL-LEFTY pathway and has been shown in animal models and human case series to produce heterotaxy and levocardia.
ZIC3-related heterotaxy is X-linked and therefore predominantly affects males; female carriers are at risk of having affected sons.

Observational data suggest a modest increase in the rate of complex congenital heart defects including laterality defects in ART pregnancies, though absolute risk remains low and the mechanism is uncertain.

riskFactors.factor: Primary ciliary dyskinesia diagnosis in the family
riskFactors.description: PCD is autosomal recessive; siblings of an affected child carry a 25% recurrence risk of PCD, of which roughly half may have situs inversus or ambiguus.

affectedDemographics: Neonates and infants — essentially all cases are diagnosed in the first year of life due to the severity of associated cardiac defects
Both sexes equally, except for ZIC3-linked heterotaxy which predominantly affects males
Children of mothers with pregestational diabetes mellitus
Individuals with a family history of heterotaxy or primary ciliary dyskinesia
Sporadic cases in otherwise healthy families — the majority of levocardia cases arise without a family history
diagnosisOverview: Levocardia is increasingly detected prenatally during routine obstetric ultrasound when the four-chamber cardiac view appears normal but abdominal organ position is abnormal, or when a fetal cardiac anomaly prompts targeted echocardiography. Postnatally, diagnosis is triggered by cyanosis, a cardiac murmur, or abnormal oxygen saturation on newborn pulse oximetry screening. The investigation pathway begins with a chest X-ray, which reveals the cardiac position and may show abnormal lung lobation or a midline liver. Echocardiography is the definitive modality for mapping intracardiac anatomy, ventricular morphology, great artery connections, and pulmonary venous drainage. Abdominal ultrasound identifies the spleen (or its absence) and the position of the liver, stomach, and abdominal aorta. Cross-sectional imaging with CT or MRI angiography is added when echocardiographic windows are limited or when detailed three-dimensional anatomy is needed before surgery. Cardiac catheterisation remains important for precise haemodynamic assessment — particularly pulmonary vascular resistance — before palliative or corrective procedures. Genetic testing for mutations in laterality genes (NODAL, ZIC3, ciliary gene panels) is offered to families for recurrence risk counselling. The major differential is simple dextrocardia with situs inversus, where the cardiac and visceral arrangements are concordant and structural heart disease is uncommon.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detailed real-time assessment of cardiac structure, position, and great artery connections from the second trimester; the primary prenatal diagnostic tool.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A specialised ultrasound performed by a paediatric cardiologist or maternal-fetal medicine specialist, usually from 18-22 weeks; the probe is applied to the mother's abdomen and the session takes 45-90 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive mapping of intracardiac anatomy, ventricular morphology, atrioventricular and ventriculoarterial connections, and pulmonary venous return in the neonate.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless gel-based ultrasound of the chest performed at the bedside; sedation is rarely needed in neonates but may be used in older infants who cannot remain still.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms cardiac position, identifies abnormal lung lobation (bilateral bilobed or trilobed lungs), and shows overall cardiac silhouette and pulmonary vascularity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single anteroposterior X-ray that takes seconds; the infant is placed on a flat detector and a brief exposure is taken.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the number and position of the spleen, liver, stomach, and great abdominal vessels to classify situs and screen for asplenia or polysplenia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless gel-based ultrasound of the abdomen, usually performed immediately after the cardiac echo in the neonatal period.

diagnosticTests.test: CT or MRI angiography of the heart and great vessels
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides three-dimensional anatomical maps of extracardiac vascular anatomy, pulmonary venous connections, and systemic venous drainage when echocardiography is insufficient for surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CT takes minutes and requires brief sedation in infants; MRI takes 30-60 minutes and requires general anaesthesia in young children. Both may use intravenous contrast.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures pulmonary artery pressure and vascular resistance to determine operability and guide the timing of Fontan-type palliation or corrective surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under general anaesthesia; thin catheters are introduced through a vein in the groin and guided to the heart. The procedure takes 1-2 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies causative mutations in NODAL, ZIC3, or ciliary genes for recurrence risk counselling and to guide testing of siblings.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood or saliva sample sent to a clinical genetics laboratory; results typically take 4-8 weeks.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of levocardia is entirely directed at the associated structural heart defects; there is no treatment for the positional anomaly itself. Management follows the principles of complex congenital heart disease surgery and is highly individualised according to the specific intracardiac anatomy. Neonates with duct-dependent circulation require immediate prostaglandin E1 infusion to maintain ductal patency until an emergency palliative procedure can be performed. The overall surgical strategy is determined by whether biventricular repair is feasible — which requires two adequate ventricles and concordant atrioventricular connections — or whether single-ventricle palliation (modified Blalock-Taussig-Thomas shunt, Glenn procedure, and ultimately Fontan completion) is the planned pathway. Biventricular repair, when possible, offers better long-term outcomes. For heterotaxy with asplenia, lifelong penicillin prophylaxis and comprehensive immunisation against encapsulated organisms (pneumococcus, meningococcus, Haemophilus influenzae type b) are mandatory from the neonatal period, because overwhelming post-splenectomy sepsis carries high mortality. Children with polysplenia and interrupted inferior vena cava may have more favourable anatomy and can sometimes achieve biventricular repair or require less complex staged palliation. Long-term cardiology follow-up is lifelong and includes surveillance for residual lesions, arrhythmias, ventricular function, and, in Fontan patients, the protein-losing enteropathy and circulatory insufficiency that can develop in the second and third decades.

medicalTreatments.name: Prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil)
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous infusion that maintains patency of the ductus arteriosus in duct-dependent levocardia, ensuring either pulmonary or systemic blood flow until surgical palliation can be performed. Initiated immediately in any cyanotic neonate when a duct-dependent lesion is suspected.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Ductal reopening or maintenance is achieved in approximately 90-95% of neonates with closed or closing ducts, providing critical time for surgical stabilisation.

medicalTreatments.name: Diuretic therapy (furosemide, spironolactone)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces pulmonary overcirculation and ventricular volume load in infants with large left-to-right shunts awaiting surgical correction, improving feeding tolerance and preventing pulmonary hypertensive crisis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces symptomatic heart failure in approximately 60-70% of infants with large shunts; used as a bridge to surgery rather than definitive treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitors (captopril, enalapril)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce afterload in neonates and infants with ventricular dysfunction or large left-to-right shunts, improving cardiac output and feeding ability while awaiting definitive repair.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest improvement in cardiac function and symptom control; efficacy data in neonatal congenital heart disease show benefit as a bridge to surgical correction.

medicalTreatments.name: Prophylactic penicillin and encapsulated-organism vaccination
medicalTreatments.description: Mandatory in asplenic patients from the neonatal period. Daily oral penicillin V (or amoxicillin) and vaccines against Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, and Haemophilus influenzae type b prevent overwhelming post-splenectomy infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Penicillin prophylaxis reduces overwhelming post-splenectomy infection risk by approximately 84% in children under 5; vaccination further substantially lowers pneumococcal disease risk.

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-arrhythmic therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Sinus node dysfunction and atrioventricular conduction anomalies are common in heterotaxy. Agents such as amiodarone or beta-blockers, and permanent pacemaker implantation, are used as needed to manage atrial tachycardias and heart block.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pharmacological control of atrial arrhythmias achieves adequate rate control in 50-70% of cases; pacemaker implantation is highly effective for symptomatic bradycardia.

surgicalOptions.name: Modified Blalock-Taussig-Thomas (BTT) shunt
surgicalOptions.description: A synthetic tube graft between the subclavian or innominate artery and the pulmonary artery provides a controlled source of pulmonary blood flow as the first palliative stage in neonates with inadequate pulmonary circulation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Surgical mortality for BTT shunt as the first palliation in complex congenital heart disease ranges from 5-15% depending on centre volume and associated anatomy.

surgicalOptions.name: Pulmonary artery banding
surgicalOptions.description: Placement of a band around the main pulmonary artery to restrict excessive pulmonary blood flow and protect the pulmonary vascular bed as a first palliative step in lesions with unrestricted pulmonary circulation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Palliative pulmonary artery banding carries a procedural mortality of approximately 5-10% in complex lesions; subsequent debanding and repair add further surgical risk.

surgicalOptions.name: Glenn procedure (bidirectional cavopulmonary anastomosis)
surgicalOptions.description: The superior vena cava is divided and connected end-to-side to the right pulmonary artery, directing superior vena caval flow directly to both lungs without passing through the heart — the second stage in single-ventricle palliation, typically at 3-6 months of age.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Surgical mortality for bidirectional Glenn in experienced centres is approximately 1-3%; long-term 10-year survival approximately 85-90% in polysplenia and 70-80% in asplenia syndrome.

surgicalOptions.name: Fontan completion
surgicalOptions.description: The inferior vena cava is connected to the pulmonary artery via a total cavopulmonary connection (lateral tunnel or extracardiac conduit), completing single-ventricle palliation by directing all systemic venous return to the lungs. Performed at 2-4 years of age.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 30-day surgical mortality below 2% in high-volume centres; 10-year survival approximately 85-90% in favourable anatomy, but long-term Fontan circulation carries risk of protein-losing enteropathy, arrhythmia, and hepatic fibrosis.

surgicalOptions.name: Biventricular repair
surgicalOptions.description: When anatomy permits two adequate ventricles and concordant connections can be achieved, complete intracardiac repair — including atrial and ventricular septal defect closure, arterial switch or Rastelli operation for transposition, and pulmonary outflow tract reconstruction — offers the best long-term haemodynamic result.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Outcomes depend on the specific anatomy; for uncomplicated transposition with levocardia undergoing arterial switch, hospital mortality is below 2% in high-volume centres; more complex repairs carry 5-15% mortality.

alternativeTreatments.name: Supportive nutritional optimisation
alternativeTreatments.description: Nasogastric or gastrostomy tube feeding is used when caloric intake is inadequate for growth due to cardiac workload; achieving target weight before surgery reduces operative risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cardiac rehabilitation and supervised exercise programmes
alternativeTreatments.description: For older children and adults after Fontan completion, graduated aerobic exercise programmes improve peak oxygen consumption and quality of life. Cardiopulmonary exercise testing guides intensity and detects early Fontan failure.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with levocardia require lifelong follow-up with a congenital cardiologist. The structural cardiac defects cannot be managed by a general practitioner alone. A paediatric cardiac surgeon at a high-volume congenital heart disease centre should plan and perform all palliative and corrective procedures. Adults who have undergone Fontan palliation need transition to adult congenital heart disease (ACHD) specialists with expertise in Fontan surveillance, arrhythmia management, and liver disease screening. Asplenic patients require ongoing infectious disease guidance for travel vaccines and antibiotic prophylaxis.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a centre designated as a high-volume congenital heart disease programme — in the UK this means a designated supraspecialist centre, and in the US a centre performing at least 300 open-heart procedures annually. The team should include a paediatric cardiologist with subspecialty interest in heterotaxy, a paediatric cardiac surgeon with experience in single-ventricle palliation, and a specialist nurse coordinator for asplenia management. For adults with repaired levocardia, look for an ACHD unit affiliated with a transplant programme given the risk of late Fontan failure.
preventionStrategies: Optimal preconception control of maternal diabetes mellitus to reduce the risk of cardiac laterality defects and all congenital heart disease.
Avoidance of teratogenic drugs including isotretinoin and high-dose vitamin A supplements during the first trimester.
Genetic counselling and cascade testing of siblings when a laterality gene mutation (NODAL, ZIC3, ciliary genes) has been identified in a proband.
Routine fetal echocardiography in subsequent pregnancies after a sibling with heterotaxy, beginning at 18-20 weeks.
Prenatal diagnosis allows delivery to be planned at a centre with immediate cardiothoracic surgical capability when complex levocardia is identified antenatally.
lifestyleModifications: Asplenic children and adults must wear medical-alert identification and carry emergency antibiotics for fever management when medical care may be delayed.
Fontan patients should avoid extreme exercise that significantly raises central venous pressure and should seek cardiology clearance before competitive sport.
Moderate aerobic exercise tailored to individual exercise capacity is encouraged and shown to improve Fontan circulation efficiency.
Regular dental hygiene and prophylactic antibiotics before dental procedures reduce bacteraemia risk in those with residual intracardiac lesions.
Avoid high-altitude travel or unpressurised aircraft without cardiology assessment if residual cyanosis is present.
Pregnancy carries high risk in women with Fontan circulation and should always be planned with specialist ACHD and obstetric input.

recommended: High-calorie, nutrient-dense feeds for infants to support growth before and after surgery
Adequate protein intake to reduce the risk of protein-losing enteropathy in Fontan patients
Salt restriction when heart failure is present or ventricular function is impaired
Good hydration to maintain venous filling in Fontan circulation, where cardiac output depends on adequate preload
avoid: Excessive fluid restriction, which worsens low cardiac output in Fontan patients
High-sodium processed foods that exacerbate fluid retention and ventricular loading
Alcohol, which depresses myocardial function and is prohibited in children and should be very limited in adults with Fontan circulation

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise capacity in levocardia depends entirely on the residual cardiac anatomy and the type of repair or palliation. Children after biventricular repair with good ventricular function may participate in most recreational activities with regular cardiology review. Those after Fontan completion typically have a reduced peak oxygen consumption (VO2 max 50-60% of predicted) and should pursue moderate aerobic exercise guided by cardiopulmonary exercise testing. Competitive high-intensity sport is generally not recommended after Fontan palliation. All patients should have an individual exercise prescription from their congenital cardiologist.
dailyManagement: Asplenic patients must take daily antibiotic prophylaxis (penicillin V or amoxicillin) and keep emergency antibiotics at home, school, and when travelling.
Parents and carers should be trained to recognise fever in asplenic children as a medical emergency requiring immediate hospital assessment.
Fontan patients require regular monitoring of oxygen saturation, weight, and liver function at a minimum of annual specialist review.
All patients should maintain up-to-date vaccination records, including all conjugate vaccines against encapsulated organisms for asplenic individuals.
Medical-alert wristbands are essential for asplenic patients in case of emergency presentation to unfamiliar healthcare providers.
prognosis: The prognosis of levocardia is determined almost entirely by the associated cardiac defects and the feasibility of repair. When biventricular repair is achieved with low residual haemodynamic burden, long-term survival approaches that of normal congenital heart disease surgery — 90-95% survival at 10 years in favourable anatomy. For the larger group requiring single-ventricle palliation via the Fontan pathway, 10-year survival is approximately 85-90% after Fontan completion in high-volume centres, but long-term Fontan circulation is associated with progressive complications including protein-losing enteropathy in 3-10% of patients, Fontan-associated liver disease with cirrhosis risk by the fourth decade, atrial arrhythmias, and late ventricular failure requiring transplantation. Heterotaxy with asplenia historically carried the worst prognosis, with 5-year survival below 50% in older series, but outcomes have improved significantly with modern surgical techniques, with 5-year survival now exceeding 70% in experienced centres. Pulmonary vascular disease developing before surgery substantially worsens outcomes by precluding Fontan completion.
recoveryTimeline: The initial hospitalisation for neonatal palliation typically lasts 2-4 weeks when uncomplicated, with a further recovery period of 4-6 weeks at home before the next planned stage. Glenn procedure at 3-6 months of age involves hospitalisation of 7-14 days. Fontan completion at 2-4 years requires 1-3 weeks of hospitalisation and several months of gradual recovery. After biventricular repair, hospitalisation is typically 7-14 days. Children generally return to normal developmental milestones after each stage with appropriate physiotherapy and developmental support. Lifelong outpatient follow-up continues thereafter.
complications: Overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) in asplenic patients — a fulminant, life-threatening bacteraemia that can progress to septic shock within hours, requiring immediate hospital treatment.

Fontan-associated liver disease — progressive hepatic fibrosis and, in some patients, cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma developing from the second decade of life due to chronically elevated central venous pressure.

Protein-losing enteropathy — loss of serum proteins into the gut in 3-10% of Fontan patients, causing peripheral oedema, pleural effusions, and recurrent infections; carries a poor prognosis without intervention.
Atrial arrhythmias — macro-re-entrant tachycardias are the most common serious late complication of the Fontan operation, occurring in up to 50% of patients by the third decade.

Ventricular dysfunction and Fontan failure — progressive decline in single-ventricle function requiring cardiac transplantation in a subset of patients, with 10-year transplant-free survival of approximately 60-70% in complex heterotaxy.
Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations — develop in some patients after Glenn or Fontan procedures when hepatic factor is excluded from the pulmonary circulation, causing worsening cyanosis.

supportResources.name: CHD UK — Congenital Heart Disease support organisation
supportResources.description: UK charity providing information, peer support, and advocacy for people living with congenital heart disease including complex heterotaxy and levocardia.

supportResources.name: Conquering CHD (formerly Pediatric Congenital Heart Association)
supportResources.description: US patient advocacy organisation offering family support, educational resources, and links to congenital heart disease specialists.

supportResources.name: Asplenia-related resources — Spleen Health
supportResources.description: NHS guidance on preventing infection in people without a functioning spleen, including vaccination schedules and emergency antibiotic guidance.

sources.title: Sutherland MJC, Ware SM. Disorders of left-right asymmetry: heterotaxy and situs inversus. Am J Med Genet Part C Semin Med Genet. 2009
Loomba RS et al. Outcomes of heterotaxy with complex congenital heart disease: single-institution experience. Pediatr Cardiol. 2016
Jacobs JP et al. The STS Congenital Heart Surgery Database: overview and current analytic strategies. World J Pediatr Congenit Heart Surg. 2012
Baban A et al. Heterotaxy syndrome: how often are right atrial isomerism and left atrial isomerism diagnosed in patients with splenic abnormalities? Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2010
NICE — Prophylaxis against infective endocarditis: antimicrobial prophylaxis against infective endocarditis in adults and children undergoing interventional procedures. NICE Clinical Guideline CG64
ACC/AHA 2018 Guidelines for the Management of Adults With Congenital Heart Disease
NHS Specialist Commissioning — Standards for Paediatric Cardiac Surgery Services

confusedWithConditions.name: Dextrocardia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dextrocardia means the heart is positioned in the right hemithorax; when accompanied by situs inversus totalis it is usually benign, unlike levocardia which almost always carries severe intracardiac defects.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tetralogy of Fallot
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tetralogy of Fallot is a specific conotruncal defect with normal cardiac position; levocardia describes the positional-visceral mismatch that may coexist with any of several structural defects including TOF.

relatedConditions.name: Dextrocardia
relatedConditions.relevance: The mirror-image cardiac malposition that contrasts with levocardia; together they encompass the spectrum of cardiac positional anomalies.

relatedConditions.name: Tetralogy of Fallot
relatedConditions.relevance: One of the most common intracardiac defects found in association with levocardia and heterotaxy.

relatedConditions.name: Patent ductus arteriosus
relatedConditions.relevance: Duct dependency is the critical neonatal emergency in many levocardia presentations requiring immediate prostaglandin therapy.

faqs.question: What is levocardia?
faqs.answer: Levocardia is a congenital condition where the heart sits in its normal left-sided position but the abdominal organs are mirror-imaged or randomly arranged. This mismatch almost always comes with serious structural heart defects that need surgical treatment.

faqs.question: Is levocardia the same as dextrocardia?
faqs.answer: No. In dextrocardia the heart is on the right side of the chest; in levocardia the heart stays on the left while the abdominal organs are abnormally arranged. When dextrocardia and situs inversus occur together the heart is usually structurally normal, whereas levocardia almost always involves severe intracardiac defects.

faqs.question: How is levocardia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Most cases are identified on fetal echocardiography when organ position looks abnormal on routine obstetric ultrasound. After birth, echocardiography, chest X-ray, and abdominal ultrasound confirm the cardiac anatomy and organ arrangement. CT or MRI angiography is added before surgery.

faqs.question: What heart defects are associated with levocardia?
faqs.answer: Nearly all cases of levocardia involve complex structural heart defects including transposition of the great arteries, pulmonary stenosis or atresia, single ventricle, atrioventricular septal defects, and total anomalous pulmonary venous connection. The specific combination varies from person to person.

faqs.question: Can levocardia be treated?
faqs.answer: There is no treatment for the positional anomaly itself. Treatment targets the associated structural heart defects through staged surgical palliation or, when anatomy allows, complete biventricular repair. Most patients require multiple operations across childhood.

faqs.question: What is the Fontan procedure and why is it used in levocardia?
faqs.answer: The Fontan procedure connects all systemic venous return directly to the pulmonary arteries, bypassing a single functional ventricle. It is the final stage of palliation for levocardia patients who cannot undergo two-ventricle repair. It is completed around age 2-4 years after preliminary Blalock-Taussig and Glenn shunts.

faqs.question: What is asplenia and why does it matter in levocardia?
faqs.answer: Asplenia means the spleen is absent, which occurs in right isomerism heterotaxy. Without a spleen, the immune system cannot effectively fight infections by encapsulated bacteria such as pneumococcus. Asplenic patients must take daily prophylactic antibiotics and have comprehensive vaccinations from birth to prevent life-threatening infection.

faqs.question: What causes levocardia?
faqs.answer: Levocardia results from disruption of the genes and cellular mechanisms that establish left-right body asymmetry in the first four weeks of embryo development. Identified causes include mutations in NODAL signalling genes, primary ciliary dyskinesia gene mutations, and ZIC3 X-linked variants. Maternal diabetes and certain teratogens also raise risk.

faqs.question: Is levocardia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most levocardia cases are sporadic with no family history. When a causative mutation is found — such as in ZIC3 or a NODAL pathway gene — recurrence risk can be 25-50% depending on inheritance pattern. Genetic counselling is recommended for all families after a diagnosis.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy for someone with levocardia?
faqs.answer: Life expectancy depends on the associated cardiac defects and surgical outcomes. With biventricular repair and good haemodynamics, survival approaches 90% at 10 years. Fontan patients have approximately 85-90% survival at 10 years but face lifelong risks including arrhythmia, liver disease, and ventricular failure. Asplenia historically worsened prognosis but modern care has improved outcomes substantially.

faqs.question: Does levocardia affect adults?
faqs.answer: Adults with levocardia have survived into adulthood either with repaired defects or with the Fontan circulation. They require lifelong specialist care at adult congenital heart disease centres, with monitoring for arrhythmias, Fontan-associated liver disease, and ventricular function. Pregnancy carries high risk and requires specialist planning.

faqs.question: What is prostaglandin E1 and why is it given to newborns with levocardia?
faqs.answer: Prostaglandin E1 keeps the ductus arteriosus open in newborns whose heart defects depend on ductal blood flow for either pulmonary or systemic circulation. Without it, ductal closure can cause rapid clinical deterioration. It is given intravenously as an emergency treatment until surgery can be performed.

faqs.question: Can levocardia be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Yes. Prenatal detection is increasingly common through routine obstetric ultrasound, which can reveal abnormal organ arrangement, and fetal echocardiography, which maps the cardiac anatomy. Prenatal diagnosis allows delivery to be planned at a centre with immediate paediatric cardiac surgical capability.

faqs.question: What is heterotaxy syndrome?
faqs.answer: Heterotaxy syndrome describes a group of conditions where the internal organs are not arranged in their normal left-right positions. Levocardia with situs ambiguus falls under this umbrella. Subtypes include asplenia (right isomerism) and polysplenia (left isomerism) syndromes, each with characteristic organ and cardiac anatomy.

faqs.question: Do levocardia patients need antibiotics for dental procedures?
faqs.answer: Patients with certain residual intracardiac defects, particularly those with unrepaired cyanotic lesions, prosthetic valves, or previous infective endocarditis, need antibiotic prophylaxis before dental procedures according to current guidelines. Asplenic patients always need prophylaxis regardless of cardiac status.

faqs.question: Can adults with levocardia have children?
faqs.answer: Many adults with repaired levocardia have successfully had children, but pregnancy carries significant risk — particularly in Fontan patients, where maternal mortality risk is elevated. Pre-conception planning with an adult congenital heart disease specialist and high-risk obstetrician is essential before attempting pregnancy.

faqs.question: What happens if levocardia is not treated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, most neonates with levocardia and complex intracardiac defects do not survive beyond the first weeks or months of life due to severe cyanosis, heart failure, or overwhelming sepsis in asplenic cases. Historical series before modern cardiac surgery showed very high early mortality.

faqs.question: Is exercise safe for people with levocardia after surgery?
faqs.answer: Moderate aerobic exercise is encouraged for most patients after surgery and is beneficial for Fontan circulation efficiency. Competitive or high-intensity sport is generally not recommended for Fontan patients. Individual exercise prescriptions based on cardiopulmonary exercise testing guide safe activity levels.

faqs.question: What is polysplenia and how does it differ from asplenia in levocardia?
faqs.answer: Polysplenia describes multiple small spleens along the right side, associated with bilateral left-sidedness (left isomerism). Asplenia means no spleen, associated with bilateral right-sidedness (right isomerism). Asplenia carries greater immunological risk and usually more severe cardiac defects than polysplenia.

faqs.question: Which hospitals specialise in treating levocardia?
faqs.answer: Levocardia requires a designated high-volume congenital heart surgery centre. In the UK, NHS England designates supraspecialist congenital cardiac centres. In the US, centres accredited by the Joint Commission with paediatric cardiac surgery programmes of at least 300 annual cases offer the best outcomes for complex heterotaxy.

metaDescription: Levocardia is a rare congenital condition where the heart is left-sided but organs are misarranged. Learn causes, diagnosis, surgery options and prognosis.
